ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chili crab meat with knife-sliced noodles. FOODON_00002572 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is used as the closest parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for chili crab noodle dishes. The noodle component is not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific Singaporean chili crab noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared serving of approximately 500 g, intended for 1–2 persons, comprising a bowl of knife-sliced noodles topped with generous crab meat in chili-tomato sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli Crab (HPB Singapore Foodo) and Knife-Cut Noodles component estimated from USDA SR Legacy wheat noodles cooked; composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Chili Crab Meat with Knife-Sliced Noodles' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining: (1) HPB Singapore Foodo chilli crab values (crab meat in chili-tomato sauce component, approximately 55% of dish weight) and (2) USDA SR Legacy cooked wheat noodles (knife-sliced noodle component, approximately 45% of dish weight). Per-100g values are weighted composites. Energy cross-checked against typical Singapore mixed seafood noodle dish profiles. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Noodles (wheat-based, cooked) GI reference; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chili crab meat with knife-sliced noodles as a composite dish. GI of 52 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate source: wheat-based knife-sliced noodles (dao xiao mian), analogous to cooked wheat noodles with GI approximately 47–58 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The crab meat and chili-tomato sauce components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. A midpoint GI of 52 is used. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 72.5) / 100 = 37.7, where carbohydrate per 500g serving = 72.5g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from chili-tomato sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving (approximately 250g) as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420mg per 100g yields over 2,000mg sodium per full shared serving, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2,000mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat-based knife-sliced noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a full 500g serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 37.7, which is high; portion control to half a serving and pairing with the protein-rich crab meat component can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from crab meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the high sodium content and moderate cholesterol (approximately 55mg per 100g from crab meat); limiting to a half-portion and requesting reduced sauce can help manage cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jumbo Chili Crab Meat with Knife-Sliced Noodles (蟹肉刀削面) is a Singaporean-Chinese fusion dish that combines two iconic culinary elements: Singapore's famous chili crab and the northern Chinese knife-sliced noodle (dao xiao mian). The dish features generous portions of crab meat — typically from mud crab or Sri Lankan crab — served in a rich, tangy, and mildly spicy chili-tomato sauce made with fresh chili, tomato, egg, garlic, ginger, and soy sauce. The noodles are hand-cut from a block of wheat dough using a blade, producing thick, irregular, chewy ribbons with a satisfying bite. The sauce clings to the noodles, delivering a complex umami-sweet-spicy flavour profile. This dish is typically served as a shared meal for 1–2 persons and is priced at the premium end of Singapore's hawker and casual dining spectrum. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate energy density of approximately 118 kcal per 100g, with meaningful protein from crab meat (approximately 8.5g per 100g) and carbohydrate from the noodles (approximately 14.5g per 100g). Sodium content is elevated due to the sauce base, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), driven by the wheat noodle component. Crab meat contributes vitamin B12, zinc, and selenium. This dish is not suitable for individuals with crustacean shellfish, gluten, egg, or soy allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chili crab noodles?
Is dao xiao mian high in carbs?
Can diabetics eat chili crab noodles?
Is chili crab noodles high in sodium?
What allergens are in chili crab noodles?
What is the GI of crab noodles?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining HPB Singapore Foodo chilli crab data with USDA SR Legacy cooked wheat noodle data in approximate proportions. GI is estimated from analogous wheat noodle data per University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation and absence of a direct dish-level measurement.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy sources used for this composite estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source; USDA SR Legacy crab values suggest selenium is present but a reliable per-100g composite figure cannot be confirmed without direct measurement. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in both crab meat and wheat noodle components per USDA SR Legacy. The dish is described as 'For 1-2 Pax' on field survey data at SGD 32.00. Halal status is not confirmed; the restaurant (The Seafood Market by House of White Bee Hoon) is not listed as a halal-certified establishment on MUIS Singapore as of the processing date. Nutrient values should be validated against a direct laboratory analysis or a future HPB Foodo composite entry if one becomes available.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed canonical English food name from input food_name removing all-caps formatting.
Added Chinese traditional form 蟹肉刀削麵 alongside simplified 蟹肉刀削面.
Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.
Estimated per-100g nutrients from HPB Singapore Foodo chilli crab and USDA SR Legacy wheat noodles composite.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Set GI to 52 based on analogous wheat noodle data from University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category set to 'low' per ≤55 threshold.
Used closest_parent match_type for FoodOn and FoodEx2 with gap notes; SNOMED CT set to none with gap note.
Verified all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Confirmed trans_fat_g 0.0 is justified (confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy) — no zero-macro justification needed as core macros are all non-zero.
Set is_halal to false as restaurant halal certification not confirmed on MUIS Singapore.
